questionText: A point moves along the path $x = 2\sin t$, $y = \cos 2t$. Show that $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -2\sin t$, and hence find the gradient of the path and the coordinates of the point when $t = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$.

steps.description: Differentiate x with respect to t
steps.explanation: Differentiating $2\sin t$ gives the horizontal velocity component.

steps.description: Differentiate y with respect to t
steps.explanation: By the chain rule, $\cos 2t$ differentiates to $-\sin 2t$ times the derivative of $2t$, giving $-2\sin 2t$.

steps.description: Form the gradient in terms of t
steps.explanation: Dividing the vertical rate by the horizontal rate gives the slope of the path, which we now simplify.
Rewriting $\sin 2t$ in terms of $\sin t$ and $\cos t$ lets a common factor of $\cos t$ cancel with the denominator.

steps.workingLatex: \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{-2(2\sin t\cos t)}{2\cos t} = \dfrac{-4\sin t\cos t}{2\cos t} = -2\sin t
steps.explanation: Cancelling the shared $\cos t$ leaves the required tidy expression $-2\sin t$ for the gradient.

steps.description: Evaluate the gradient at t = pi/6
steps.explanation: Substituting the exact value $\sin\tfrac{\pi}{6}=\tfrac12$ gives a gradient of $-1$ at that instant.
Putting $t=\tfrac{\pi}{6}$ into the horizontal position equation gives $x=1$.
Putting $t=\tfrac{\pi}{6}$ into the vertical position equation gives $y=\tfrac12$.
So at this moment the point is at $\left(1,\tfrac12\right)$ and the path is sloping downwards with gradient $-1$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -2\sin t$; at $t = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$ the gradient is $-1$ and the point is $\left(1,\dfrac{1}{2}\right)$.
